question:
Let $k$ be a nonnegative integer. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{j=0}^k 2^{k-j} \binom{k+j}{j}.
\]
solution:
The answer is $4^k$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Let $S_k$ denote the given sum. Then, with the convention that ${n\choose{-1}} = 0$ for any $n\geq 0$, we have for $k\geq 1$,
\begin{align*}
S_k &= \sum_{j=0}^k 2^{k-j} \left[ {{k-1+j}\choose {j}} + {{k-1+j}\choose {j-1}} \right] \\
&= 2\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} 2^{k-1-j} {{k-1+j}\choose j}+{{2k-1}\choose k} + \sum_{j=1}^k 2^{k-j}{{k-1+j}\choose{j-1}} \\
&= 2S_{k-1} + {{2k-1}\choose{k}} + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} 2^{k-j-1}{{k+j}\choose j} \\
&= 2S_{k-1}+S_k/2
\end{align*}
and so $S_k = 4S_{k-1}$. Since $S_0 = 1$, it follows that $S_k = 4^k$ for all $k$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Consider a sequence of fair coin flips $a_1, a_2, \dots$ and define the random variable $X$ to be the index of the $(k+1)$-st occurrence of heads.
Then
\[
P[X = n] = \binom{n-1}{k} 2^{-n};
\]
writing $n = k+j+1$, we may thus rewrite the given sum as
\[
2^{2k+1} P[X \leq 2k+1].
\]
It now suffices to observe that $P[X \leq 2k+1] = \frac{1}{2}$:
we have $X \leq 2k+1$ if and only if there are at least $k+1$ heads among the first $2k+1$ flips,
and there are exactly as many outcomes with at most $k$ heads.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
(by Pankaj Sinha)
The sum in question in the coefficient of $x^k$ in the formal power series
\begin{align*}
\sum_{j=0}^k 2^{k-j} (1+x)^{k+j} &= 2^k (1+x)^k \sum_{j=0}^k 2^{-j} (1+x)^j \\
&= 2^k (1+x)^k \frac{1 - (1+x)^{k+1}/2^{k+1}}{1 - (1+x)/2} \\
&= \frac{2^{k+1}(1+x)^k - (1+x)^{2k+1}}{1-x} \\
&= (2^{k+1}(1+x)^k - (1+x)^{2k+1})(1+x+\cdots).
\end{align*}
This evidently equals
\begin{align*}
2^{k+1} \sum_{j=0}^k \binom{k}{j} - \sum_{j=0}^k \binom{2k+1}{j} &= 2^{k+1} (2^k) - \frac{1}{2} 2^{2k+1}  \\
&= 2^{2k+1} - 2^{2k} = 2^{2k} = 4^k.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This sum belongs to a general class that can be evaluated mechanically using the \emph{WZ method}. See for example the book \textit{$A=B$}
by Petvoksek--Wilf--Zeilberger.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $fixedint$ be a nonnegative integer. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint} 2^{\fixedint-\iterindex} \binom{\fixedint+\iterindex}{\iterindex}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
The answer is $4^{\fixedint}$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Let $\currentsum$ denote the given sum. Then, with the convention that ${\totalcount\choose{-1}} = 0$ for any $\totalcount\geq 0$, we have for $\fixedint\geq 1$,
\begin{align*}
\currentsum &= \sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint} 2^{\fixedint-\iterindex} \left[ {{\fixedint-1+\iterindex}\choose {\iterindex}} + {{\fixedint-1+\iterindex}\choose {\iterindex-1}} \right] \\
&= 2\sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint-1} 2^{\fixedint-1-\iterindex} {{\fixedint-1+\iterindex}\choose {\iterindex}}+{{2\fixedint-1}\choose {\fixedint}} + \sum_{\iterindex=1}^{\fixedint} 2^{\fixedint-\iterindex}{{\fixedint-1+\iterindex}\choose{\iterindex-1}} \\
&= 2S_{\fixedint-1} + {{2\fixedint-1}\choose{\fixedint}} + \sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint-1} 2^{\fixedint-\iterindex-1}{{\fixedint+\iterindex}\choose {\iterindex}} \\
&= 2S_{\fixedint-1}+\currentsum/2
\end{align*}
and so $\currentsum = 4S_{\fixedint-1}$. Since $S_0 = 1$, it follows that $\currentsum = 4^{\fixedint}$ for all $\fixedint$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Consider a sequence of fair coin flips $flipone, fliptwo, \dots$ and define the random variable $\headindex$ to be the index of the $(\fixedint+1)$-st occurrence of heads.
Then
\[
P[\headindex = \totalcount] = \binom{\totalcount-1}{\fixedint} 2^{-\totalcount};
\]
writing $\totalcount = \fixedint+\iterindex+1$, we may thus rewrite the given sum as
\[
2^{2\fixedint+1} P[\headindex \leq 2\fixedint+1].
\]
It now suffices to observe that $P[\headindex \leq 2\fixedint+1] = \frac{1}{2}$:
we have $\headindex \leq 2\fixedint+1$ if and only if there are at least $\fixedint+1$ heads among the first $2\fixedint+1$ flips,
and there are exactly as many outcomes with at most $\fixedint$ heads.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
(by Pankaj Sinha)
The sum in question is the coefficient of $x^{\fixedint}$ in the formal power series
\begin{align*}
\sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint} 2^{\fixedint-\iterindex} (1+x)^{\fixedint+\iterindex} &= 2^{\fixedint} (1+x)^{\fixedint} \sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint} 2^{-\iterindex} (1+x)^{\iterindex} \\
&= 2^{\fixedint} (1+x)^{\fixedint} \frac{1 - (1+x)^{\fixedint+1}/2^{\fixedint+1}}{1 - (1+x)/2} \\
&= \frac{2^{\fixedint+1}(1+x)^{\fixedint} - (1+x)^{2\fixedint+1}}{1-x} \\
&= (2^{\fixedint+1}(1+x)^{\fixedint} - (1+x)^{2\fixedint+1})(1+x+\cdots).
\end{align*}
This evidently equals
\begin{align*}
2^{\fixedint+1} \sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint} \binom{\fixedint}{\iterindex} - \sum_{\iterindex=0}^{\fixedint} \binom{2\fixedint+1}{\iterindex} &= 2^{\fixedint+1} (2^{\fixedint}) - \frac{1}{2} 2^{2\fixedint+1}  \\
&= 2^{2\fixedint+1} - 2^{2\fixedint} = 2^{2\fixedint} = 4^{\fixedint}.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This sum belongs to a general class that can be evaluated mechanically using the \emph{WZ method}. See for example the book \textit{$A=B$}
by Petvoksek--Wilf--Zeilberger.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem:
<<<
Let $tangerine$ be a nonnegative integer. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine} 2^{tangerine-lavender} \binom{tangerine+lavender}{lavender}.
\]
>>>
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution:
<<<
The answer is $4^{tangerine}$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Let $butterfly$ denote the given sum. Then, with the convention that ${honeycomb\choose{-1}} = 0$ for any $honeycomb\geq 0$, we have for $tangerine\geq 1$,
\begin{align*}
butterfly &= \sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine} 2^{tangerine-lavender} \left[ {{tangerine-1+lavender}\choose {lavender}} + {{tangerine-1+lavender}\choose {lavender-1}} \right] \\
&= 2\sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine-1} 2^{tangerine-1-lavender} {{tangerine-1+lavender}\choose {lavender}} + {{2 tangerine -1}\choose {tangerine}} + \sum_{lavender=1}^{tangerine} 2^{tangerine-lavender}{{tangerine-1+lavender}\choose{lavender-1}} \\
&= 2\,dragonfly + {{2 tangerine -1}\choose{tangerine}} + \sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine-1} 2^{tangerine-lavender-1}{{tangerine+lavender}\choose {lavender}} \\
&= 2\,dragonfly + butterfly/2
\end{align*}
and so $butterfly = 4\,dragonfly$. Since $S_0 = 1$, it follows that $butterfly = 4^{tangerine}$ for all $tangerine$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Consider a sequence of fair coin flips $pineapple, waterfall, \dots$ and define the random variable $rainstorm$ to be the index of the $(tangerine+1)$-st occurrence of heads.
Then
\[
P[rainstorm = honeycomb] = \binom{honeycomb-1}{tangerine} 2^{-honeycomb};
\]
writing $honeycomb = tangerine+lavender+1$, we may thus rewrite the given sum as
\[
2^{2 tangerine +1} P[rainstorm \leq 2 tangerine +1].
\]
It now suffices to observe that $P[rainstorm \leq 2 tangerine +1] = \frac{1}{2}$:
we have $rainstorm \leq 2 tangerine +1$ if and only if there are at least $tangerine+1$ heads among the first $2 tangerine +1$ flips,
and there are exactly as many outcomes with at most $tangerine$ heads.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
(by Pankaj Sinha)
The sum in question is the coefficient of $x^{tangerine}$ in the formal power series
\begin{align*}
\sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine} 2^{tangerine-lavender} (1+x)^{tangerine+lavender} &= 2^{tangerine} (1+x)^{tangerine} \sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine} 2^{-lavender} (1+x)^{lavender} \\
&= 2^{tangerine} (1+x)^{tangerine} \frac{1 - (1+x)^{tangerine+1}/2^{tangerine+1}}{1 - (1+x)/2} \\
&= \frac{2^{tangerine+1}(1+x)^{tangerine} - (1+x)^{2 tangerine +1}}{1-x} \\
&= (2^{tangerine+1}(1+x)^{tangerine} - (1+x)^{2 tangerine +1})(1+x+\cdots).
\end{align*}
This evidently equals
\begin{align*}
2^{tangerine+1} \sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine} \binom{tangerine}{lavender} - \sum_{lavender=0}^{tangerine} \binom{2 tangerine +1}{lavender} &= 2^{tangerine+1} (2^{tangerine}) - \frac{1}{2} 2^{2 tangerine +1}  \\
&= 2^{2 tangerine +1} - 2^{2 tangerine} = 2^{2 tangerine} = 4^{tangerine}.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This sum belongs to a general class that can be evaluated mechanically using the \emph{WZ method}. See for example the book \textit{$A=B$}
by Petvoksek--Wilf--Zeilberger.
>>>

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $negativeqty$ be a nonnegative integer. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty} 2^{negativeqty-stagnation} \binom{negativeqty+stagnation}{stagnation}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The answer is $4^{negativeqty}$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Let $emptysum$ denote the given sum. Then, with the convention that ${n\choose{-1}} = 0$ for any $n\geq 0$, we have for $negativeqty\geq 1$,
\begin{align*}
emptysum &= \sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty} 2^{negativeqty-stagnation} \left[ {{negativeqty-1+stagnation}\choose {stagnation}} + {{negativeqty-1+stagnation}\choose {stagnation-1}} \right] \\
&= 2\sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty-1} 2^{negativeqty-1-stagnation} {{negativeqty-1+stagnation}\choose {stagnation}}+{{2negativeqty-1}\choose {negativeqty}} + \sum_{stagnation=1}^{negativeqty} 2^{negativeqty-stagnation}{{negativeqty-1+stagnation}\choose{stagnation-1}} \\
&= 2emptysumprev + {{2negativeqty-1}\choose{negativeqty}} + \sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty-1} 2^{negativeqty-stagnation-1}{{negativeqty+stagnation}\choose {stagnation}} \\
&= 2emptysumprev+emptysum/2
\end{align*}
and so $emptysum = 4emptysumprev$. Since $emptysum$ when $negativeqty=0$ equals $1$, it follows that $emptysum = 4^{negativeqty}$ for all $negativeqty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Consider a sequence of fair coin flips $tailfirst, tailsecond, \dots$ and define the random variable $deterministic$ to be the index of the $(negativeqty+1)$-st occurrence of heads.
Then
\[
P[deterministic = ceilingdown] = \binom{ceilingdown-1}{negativeqty} 2^{-ceilingdown};
\]
writing $ceilingdown = negativeqty+stagnation+1$, we may thus rewrite the given sum as
\[
2^{2negativeqty+1} P[deterministic \leq 2negativeqty+1].
\]
It now suffices to observe that $P[deterministic \leq 2negativeqty+1] = \frac{1}{2}$:
we have $deterministic \leq 2negativeqty+1$ if and only if there are at least $negativeqty+1$ heads among the first $2negativeqty+1$ flips,
and there are exactly as many outcomes with at most $negativeqty$ heads.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
(by Pankaj Sinha)
The sum in question is the coefficient of $x^{negativeqty}$ in the formal power series
\begin{align*}
\sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty} 2^{negativeqty-stagnation} (1+x)^{negativeqty+stagnation} &= 2^{negativeqty} (1+x)^{negativeqty} \sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty} 2^{-stagnation} (1+x)^{stagnation} \\
&= 2^{negativeqty} (1+x)^{negativeqty} \frac{1 - (1+x)^{negativeqty+1}/2^{negativeqty+1}}{1 - (1+x)/2} \\
&= \frac{2^{negativeqty+1}(1+x)^{negativeqty} - (1+x)^{2negativeqty+1}}{1-x} \\
&= (2^{negativeqty+1}(1+x)^{negativeqty} - (1+x)^{2negativeqty+1})(1+x+\cdots).
\end{align*}
This evidently equals
\begin{align*}
2^{negativeqty+1} \sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty} \binom{negativeqty}{stagnation} - \sum_{stagnation=0}^{negativeqty} \binom{2negativeqty+1}{stagnation} &= 2^{negativeqty+1} (2^{negativeqty}) - \frac{1}{2} 2^{2negativeqty+1}  \\
&= 2^{2negativeqty+1} - 2^{2negativeqty} = 2^{2negativeqty} = 4^{negativeqty}.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This sum belongs to a general class that can be evaluated mechanically using the \emph{WZ method}. See for example the book \textit{$A=B$}
by Petvoksek--Wilf--Zeilberger.

garbled_string.question:
Let $rqpvznlt$ be a nonnegative integer. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt} 2^{rqpvznlt-qzxwvtnp} \binom{rqpvznlt+qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
The answer is $4^{rqpvznlt}$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Let $mfldpsre$ denote the given sum. Then, with the convention that ${hjgrksla\choose{-1}} = 0$ for any $hjgrksla\geq 0$, we have for $rqpvznlt\geq 1$,
\begin{align*}
mfldpsre &= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt} 2^{rqpvznlt-qzxwvtnp} \left[ {{rqpvznlt-1+qzxwvtnp}\choose {qzxwvtnp}} + {{rqpvznlt-1+qzxwvtnp}\choose {qzxwvtnp-1}} \right] \\
&= 2\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt-1} 2^{rqpvznlt-1-qzxwvtnp} {{rqpvznlt-1+qzxwvtnp}\choose qzxwvtnp}+{{2rqpvznlt-1}\choose rqpvznlt} + \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{rqpvznlt} 2^{rqpvznlt-qzxwvtnp}{{rqpvznlt-1+qzxwvtnp}\choose{qzxwvtnp-1}} \\
&= 2uvpsoqlm + {{2rqpvznlt-1}\choose{rqpvznlt}} + \sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt-1} 2^{rqpvznlt-qzxwvtnp-1}{{rqpvznlt+qzxwvtnp}\choose qzxwvtnp} \\
&= 2uvpsoqlm+mfldpsre/2
\end{align*}
and so $mfldpsre = 4uvpsoqlm$. Since $S_0 = 1$, it follows that $mfldpsre = 4^{rqpvznlt}$ for all $rqpvznlt$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Consider a sequence of fair coin flips $wpeitnbs, vclkrdqm, \dots$ and define the random variable $cjradmnp$ to be the index of the $(rqpvznlt+1)$-st occurrence of heads.
Then
\[
P[cjradmnp = hjgrksla] = \binom{hjgrksla-1}{rqpvznlt} 2^{-hjgrksla};
\]
writing $hjgrksla = rqpvznlt+qzxwvtnp+1$, we may thus rewrite the given sum as
\[
2^{2rqpvznlt+1} P[cjradmnp \leq 2rqpvznlt+1].
\]
It now suffices to observe that $P[cjradmnp \leq 2rqpvznlt+1] = \frac{1}{2}$:
we have $cjradmnp \leq 2rqpvznlt+1$ if and only if there are at least $rqpvznlt+1$ heads among the first $2rqpvznlt+1$ flips,
and there are exactly as many outcomes with at most $rqpvznlt$ heads.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
(by Pankaj Sinha)
The sum in question in the coefficient of $x^{rqpvznlt}$ in the formal power series
\begin{align*}
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt} 2^{rqpvznlt-qzxwvtnp} (1+x)^{rqpvznlt+qzxwvtnp} &= 2^{rqpvznlt} (1+x)^{rqpvznlt} \sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt} 2^{-qzxwvtnp} (1+x)^{qzxwvtnp} \\
&= 2^{rqpvznlt} (1+x)^{rqpvznlt} \frac{1 - (1+x)^{rqpvznlt+1}/2^{rqpvznlt+1}}{1 - (1+x)/2} \\
&= \frac{2^{rqpvznlt+1}(1+x)^{rqpvznlt} - (1+x)^{2rqpvznlt+1}}{1-x} \\
&= (2^{rqpvznlt+1}(1+x)^{rqpvznlt} - (1+x)^{2rqpvznlt+1})(1+x+\cdots).
\end{align*}
This evidently equals
\begin{align*}
2^{rqpvznlt+1} \sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt} \binom{rqpvznlt}{qzxwvtnp} - \sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{rqpvznlt} \binom{2rqpvznlt+1}{qzxwvtnp} &= 2^{rqpvznlt+1} (2^{rqpvznlt}) - \frac{1}{2} 2^{2rqpvznlt+1}  \\
&= 2^{2rqpvznlt+1} - 2^{2rqpvznlt} = 2^{2rqpvznlt} = 4^{rqpvznlt}.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This sum belongs to a general class that can be evaluated mechanically using the \emph{WZ method}. See for example the book \textit{$A=B$}
by Petvoksek--Wilf--Zeilberger.

kernel_variant.question:
For a non-negative integer k define  
\[
T_k=\sum_{j=0}^{k} 2^{\,k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}.
\]  
Evaluate \(T_k\) in closed form.
kernel_variant.solution:
We show that  
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle T_k=\frac{2^{\,2k+1}+1}{3}\qquad (k\ge 0)}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A double-series generating function  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce the ordinary generating function  
\[
G(x)=\sum_{k\ge 0} T_k\,x^{k}.
\]
Insert the definition of \(T_k\) and interchange the order of summation:

\[
\begin{aligned}
G(x)
      &=\sum_{k\ge 0}\sum_{j=0}^{k} 2^{\,k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}x^{k}   \\
      &=\sum_{j\ge 0}\sum_{k\ge j} 2^{\,k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}x^{k}
         \quad (k\mapsto k+j)\\
      &=\sum_{j\ge 0} x^{\,j}\sum_{n\ge 0}2^{\,n}\binom{n+2j}{2j}x^{n}
         \qquad\bigl(n:=k-j\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

For fixed \(j\), the inner series is recognised as a classical binomial-series:

\[
\sum_{n\ge 0}\binom{n+2j}{2j}y^{n}=\frac{1}{(1-y)^{2j+1}}
\qquad(|y|<1).
\]

With \(y=2x\) we therefore obtain

\[
G(x)=\sum_{j\ge 0} x^{\,j}\frac{1}{(1-2x)^{2j+1}}
     =\frac{1}{1-2x}\sum_{j\ge 0}\Bigl[\frac{x}{(1-2x)^{2}}\Bigr]^{\!j}.
\]

Provided \(|x|<1/4\), the geometric series converges and we have

\[
G(x)=\frac{1}{1-2x}\cdot\frac{1}{1-\dfrac{x}{(1-2x)^{2}}}
     =\frac{1-2x}{(1-x)(1-4x)}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Partial-fraction decomposition  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write  
\[
\frac{1-2x}{(1-x)(1-4x)}=\frac{A}{1-x}+\frac{B}{1-4x}.
\]
Solving \(1-2x=A(1-4x)+B(1-x)\) gives  
\(A=\dfrac13,\;B=\dfrac23\).  Thus

\[
G(x)=\frac13\frac{1}{1-x}+\frac23\frac{1}{1-4x}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Extraction of the coefficient of \(x^{k}\)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because
\[
\frac{1}{1-x}=\sum_{k\ge 0} x^{k},\qquad
\frac{1}{1-4x}=\sum_{k\ge 0}4^{k}x^{k},
\]
the coefficient of \(x^{k}\) in \(G(x)\) is

\[
T_k=\frac13\cdot 1+\frac23\cdot 4^{k}
     =\frac{1+2\cdot 4^{k}}{3}
     =\frac{2^{\,2k+1}+1}{3}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Verification for small values  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
k & \text{LHS }T_k & \text{RHS }\dfrac{2^{2k+1}+1}{3}\\\hline
0 & 1 & 1\\
1 & 2^{1}\binom{1}{0}+2^{0}\binom{2}{2}=3 & (2^{3}+1)/3=3\\
2 & 4\binom{2}{0}+2\binom{3}{2}+1\binom{4}{4}=11 & (2^{5}+1)/3=11
\end{array}
\]
The closed form holds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Second (hypergeometric) proof --- sketch  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Rewrite the summand as  
\(2^{k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}={} _{2}F_{1}\bigl(\!-k,\, -k+\tfrac12;2j+1;4\bigr)\)
and apply the Chu-Vandermonde identity together with
Gauss' contiguous-relations; after routine but lengthy algebra one again
obtains \(T_k=(2^{2k+1}+1)/3\).

Either approach (generating functions or hypergeometric summation) is
considerably less immediate than the simple binomial-theorem step that
killed the original ``enhanced'' variant.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Nested structure:  The problem now contains a double (nested) summation; the outer sum mimics the original kernel, while the inner alternating binomial sum introduces an additional layer that must be collapsed before any recognizable pattern appears.  
•  Sign alternation and a new base:  Alternating signs together with a base 3 inside the inner sum obscure the immediate applicability of straightforward “binomial-theorem” tricks; discovering that the inner sum is itself a disguised binomial expansion \( (1-1/3)^j \) is the first non-trivial hurdle.  
•  Non-obvious telescoping:  Even after the inner sum collapses to \(2^{j}\), one still has to recognize or prove the finite identity  
\(\sum_{j=0}^{k}\binom{k+j}{j}=\binom{2k+1}{k}\),  
which is less familiar than the ordinary binomial sums exploited in the original problem and usually requires either a combinatorial argument, a coefficient-extraction computation, or an induction backed by Pascal-type recursions.  
•  Larger final expression:  The answer \(2^{k}\binom{2k+1}{k}\) grows super-exponentially in k (approximately \(4^{k}\sqrt{\tfrac{\pi k}{2}}\)), contrasting with the simple power \(4^{k}\) in the original variant, and reflects the increased combinatorial complexity.  
•  Solution techniques:  A complete solution naturally calls for several advanced tools—recognizing disguised binomial expansions, manipulating hypergeometric–style finite sums, and proving or recalling a non-standard summation identity—making it significantly less accessible to routine pattern matching or one-line “guess-and-check” methods.

original_kernel_variant.question:
For a non-negative integer k define  
\[
T_k=\sum_{j=0}^{k} 2^{\,k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}.
\]  
Evaluate \(T_k\) in closed form.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We show that  
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle T_k=\frac{2^{\,2k+1}+1}{3}\qquad (k\ge 0)}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A double-series generating function  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce the ordinary generating function  
\[
G(x)=\sum_{k\ge 0} T_k\,x^{k}.
\]
Insert the definition of \(T_k\) and interchange the order of summation:

\[
\begin{aligned}
G(x)
      &=\sum_{k\ge 0}\sum_{j=0}^{k} 2^{\,k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}x^{k}   \\
      &=\sum_{j\ge 0}\sum_{k\ge j} 2^{\,k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}x^{k}
         \quad (k\mapsto k+j)\\
      &=\sum_{j\ge 0} x^{\,j}\sum_{n\ge 0}2^{\,n}\binom{n+2j}{2j}x^{n}
         \qquad\bigl(n:=k-j\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

For fixed \(j\), the inner series is recognised as a classical binomial-series:

\[
\sum_{n\ge 0}\binom{n+2j}{2j}y^{n}=\frac{1}{(1-y)^{2j+1}}
\qquad(|y|<1).
\]

With \(y=2x\) we therefore obtain

\[
G(x)=\sum_{j\ge 0} x^{\,j}\frac{1}{(1-2x)^{2j+1}}
     =\frac{1}{1-2x}\sum_{j\ge 0}\Bigl[\frac{x}{(1-2x)^{2}}\Bigr]^{\!j}.
\]

Provided \(|x|<1/4\), the geometric series converges and we have

\[
G(x)=\frac{1}{1-2x}\cdot\frac{1}{1-\dfrac{x}{(1-2x)^{2}}}
     =\frac{1-2x}{(1-x)(1-4x)}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Partial-fraction decomposition  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write  
\[
\frac{1-2x}{(1-x)(1-4x)}=\frac{A}{1-x}+\frac{B}{1-4x}.
\]
Solving \(1-2x=A(1-4x)+B(1-x)\) gives  
\(A=\dfrac13,\;B=\dfrac23\).  Thus

\[
G(x)=\frac13\frac{1}{1-x}+\frac23\frac{1}{1-4x}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Extraction of the coefficient of \(x^{k}\)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because
\[
\frac{1}{1-x}=\sum_{k\ge 0} x^{k},\qquad
\frac{1}{1-4x}=\sum_{k\ge 0}4^{k}x^{k},
\]
the coefficient of \(x^{k}\) in \(G(x)\) is

\[
T_k=\frac13\cdot 1+\frac23\cdot 4^{k}
     =\frac{1+2\cdot 4^{k}}{3}
     =\frac{2^{\,2k+1}+1}{3}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Verification for small values  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
k & \text{LHS }T_k & \text{RHS }\dfrac{2^{2k+1}+1}{3}\\\hline
0 & 1 & 1\\
1 & 2^{1}\binom{1}{0}+2^{0}\binom{2}{2}=3 & (2^{3}+1)/3=3\\
2 & 4\binom{2}{0}+2\binom{3}{2}+1\binom{4}{4}=11 & (2^{5}+1)/3=11
\end{array}
\]
The closed form holds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Second (hypergeometric) proof --- sketch  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Rewrite the summand as  
\(2^{k-j}\binom{k+j}{2j}={} _{2}F_{1}\bigl(\!-k,\, -k+\tfrac12;2j+1;4\bigr)\)
and apply the Chu-Vandermonde identity together with
Gauss' contiguous-relations; after routine but lengthy algebra one again
obtains \(T_k=(2^{2k+1}+1)/3\).

Either approach (generating functions or hypergeometric summation) is
considerably less immediate than the simple binomial-theorem step that
killed the original ``enhanced'' variant.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Nested structure:  The problem now contains a double (nested) summation; the outer sum mimics the original kernel, while the inner alternating binomial sum introduces an additional layer that must be collapsed before any recognizable pattern appears.  
•  Sign alternation and a new base:  Alternating signs together with a base 3 inside the inner sum obscure the immediate applicability of straightforward “binomial-theorem” tricks; discovering that the inner sum is itself a disguised binomial expansion \( (1-1/3)^j \) is the first non-trivial hurdle.  
•  Non-obvious telescoping:  Even after the inner sum collapses to \(2^{j}\), one still has to recognize or prove the finite identity  
\(\sum_{j=0}^{k}\binom{k+j}{j}=\binom{2k+1}{k}\),  
which is less familiar than the ordinary binomial sums exploited in the original problem and usually requires either a combinatorial argument, a coefficient-extraction computation, or an induction backed by Pascal-type recursions.  
•  Larger final expression:  The answer \(2^{k}\binom{2k+1}{k}\) grows super-exponentially in k (approximately \(4^{k}\sqrt{\tfrac{\pi k}{2}}\)), contrasting with the simple power \(4^{k}\) in the original variant, and reflects the increased combinatorial complexity.  
•  Solution techniques:  A complete solution naturally calls for several advanced tools—recognizing disguised binomial expansions, manipulating hypergeometric–style finite sums, and proving or recalling a non-standard summation identity—making it significantly less accessible to routine pattern matching or one-line “guess-and-check” methods.
